note_data.title: Databases
keyPoints: A **database** is a collection of related data organised in a structured way to allow efficient storage and retrieval
A **table** consists of rows (records) and columns (fields) where each row represents one entity and each column represents one attribute
A **primary key** is a unique identifier for each record in a table that cannot be null or duplicated
**SQL SELECT** statement retrieves specific data from one or more tables in a database
**WHERE clause** filters records based on specified conditions to limit the results returned
**ORDER BY** clause sorts the retrieved data in ascending (ASC) or descending (DESC) order based on specified fields
**COUNT function** returns the number of records that match the specified criteria in a query
**SUM function** calculates the total of all values in a specified numeric field

definitions.definition: A collection of related data organised in a structured way that allows efficient storage, retrieval and manipulation of information
A field or combination of fields that uniquely identifies each record in a table and cannot contain null or duplicate values
Structured Query Language - a programming language used to manage and manipulate data stored in relational databases
A request for specific data from a database using SQL commands to retrieve, insert, update or delete records

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
For SQL questions, write the exact syntax with correct spelling and punctuation

modelAnswer.question: A school database contains a table called STUDENTS with fields: StudentID, Name, Class, Age, Marks. Write an SQL statement to display the names of all students in class 10A with marks greater than 75, sorted by marks in descending order.
modelAnswer.answer: SELECT Name FROM STUDENTS WHERE Class = '10A' AND Marks > 75 ORDER BY Marks DESC;
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct SELECT Name FROM STUDENTS
Mark 2: Correct WHERE Class = '10A' AND Marks > 75

commonMistakes: Never write 'WHERE Class = 10A' — always use quotes for text values 'WHERE Class = '10A''
Students often forget the semicolon at the end of SQL statements
Writing 'SORT BY' instead of 'ORDER BY' loses marks
Confusing COUNT(*) which counts all records with COUNT(fieldname) which counts non-null values in that field
Using = instead of > or < for numerical comparisons in WHERE clauses
examinerTips: SQL questions appear in every Paper 2 - practice the exact syntax
Always check spelling of SQL keywords - they must be exact
Remember to use quotes around text values but not around numbers
When using ORDER BY, specify ASC or DESC even though ASC is default
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Database structure diagram showing tables with field names, data types, and primary key indicators. Include relationship lines between tables if multiple tables are involved.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to write SQL statements to retrieve information from a database using SELECT and WHERE clauses with boolean conditions
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P2 Q5 (6 marks) — they asked students to identify data types for database fields and suggest three possible queries that could be performed on a music database
This topic was tested in M/J 2024 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — they asked students to identify foreign keys and specify which database tables they are found in, testing understanding of table relationships

This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q4 (7 marks) — they asked students to complete definitions of database terminology including fields, entities, and foreign keys with specific examples from a given database schema

workedExample.question: A music database contains a table called SONGS with fields: SongNumber, Title, Recorded, Minutes. (a) Identify the data type for each field (4 marks). (b) Write three different queries that could be performed on this database (3 marks).
steps: Step 1: Analyze each field name to determine what type of data it stores
Step 2: For SongNumber - could be text/alphanumeric as it may contain letters and numbers
Step 3: For Title - text/alphanumeric as song titles contain words
Step 4: For Recorded - date/time as this stores when the song was recorded
Step 5: For Minutes - real/numeric as this stores decimal values for song length
Step 6: Think of three different types of queries: search, calculate, and sort operations
workedExample.answer: (a) SongNumber: Text/Alphanumeric, Title: Text/Alphanumeric, Recorded: Date/Time, Minutes: Real/Numeric (b) Find all songs recorded after a specific date; Calculate total minutes of all songs; Sort songs by title alphabetically
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for each correct data type identification. 1 mark for each realistic and different query type that uses the available fields appropriately.

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused primary and foreign keys' — always remember that a foreign key in one table is the primary key in another table, creating the relationship
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often wrote incomplete SQL statements' — always include the table name after FROM and ensure WHERE conditions use correct field names and syntax
Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates frequently gave vague database terminology definitions' — always provide specific examples from the given database scenario when defining fields, entities, or keys
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a two-table database relationship diagram showing: Table 1 with primary key highlighted, Table 2 with foreign key highlighted, and an arrow connecting the foreign key to the primary key it references. Include field names, data types, and show sample data in at least 3 records per table. Mark primary keys with 'PK' and foreign keys with 'FK' annotations.
note_data.quickRecap: Databases store data in tables with rows (records) and columns (fields). Primary keys uniquely identify records, foreign keys link tables together. SQL SELECT statements retrieve data using WHERE clauses for conditions. Data types include text/alphanumeric, numeric/real, date/time, and boolean - choose based on what the field stores.
